title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Article 58
excerpt: The place of supply of the following services to a non-taxable person shall be the place where that person is established, has his permanent address or usually resides: (a) telecommunications services; (b) radio and television broadcasting services; (c) electronically supplied services, in particular those referred to in Annex II. Where the supplier of a service and the customer communicate via electronic mail, that shall not of itself mean that the service supplied is an electronically supplied service.
confirms: B2C electronically supplied services are supplied where the customer is established, has a permanent address or usually resides.
notes: Verified against the consolidated text dated 14 April 2025 (CELEX 02006L0112-20250414), not the original 2006 text.

title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Article 44
excerpt: The place of supply of services to a taxable person acting as such shall be the place where that person has established his business. However, if those services are provided to a fixed establishment of the taxable person located in a place other than the place where he has established his business, the place of supply of those services shall be the place where that fixed establishment is located. In the absence of such place of establishment or fixed establishment, the place of supply of services shall be the place where the taxable person who receives such services has his permanent address or usually resides.
confirms: Under the B2B general rule, services are supplied where the taxable customer is established or at the relevant fixed establishment.
notes: Verified against the consolidated text dated 14 April 2025.

title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Article 45
excerpt: The place of supply of services to a non-taxable person shall be the place where the supplier has established his business. However, if those services are provided from a fixed establishment of the supplier located in a place other than the place where he has established his business, the place of supply of those services shall be the place where that fixed establishment is located. In the absence of such place of establishment or fixed establishment, the place of supply of services shall be the place where the supplier has his permanent address or usually resides.
confirms: Article 45 sets the B2C general rule: services supplied to a non-taxable person are supplied where the supplier has established his business, subject to the specific exceptions provided by the Directive.
notes: Do not apply Article 45 mechanically where a specific rule may override it; keep the human-services conclusion at professional review.

title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Article 196
excerpt: VAT shall be payable by any taxable person, or non-taxable legal person identified for VAT purposes, to whom the services referred to in Article 44 are supplied, if the services are supplied by a taxable person not established within the territory of the Member State.
confirms: For Article 44 services supplied by a supplier not established in the Member State, the taxable customer is liable for VAT under reverse charge.
notes: German national implementation is cross-referenced in S12.

title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Article 226 invoice details
excerpt: (4) the customer's VAT identification number, as referred to in Article 214, under which the customer received a supply of goods or services in respect of which he is liable for payment of VAT, or received a supply of goods as referred to in Article 138; […] (11a) where the customer is liable for the payment of the VAT, the mention ‘Reverse charge’;
confirms: A reverse-charge invoice includes the customer's VAT identification number and the mention ‘Reverse charge’.
notes: The ellipsis joins two verbatim, non-contiguous points of Article 226.

title: Council Directive 2006/112/EC (consolidated), Annex II
excerpt: (1) Website supply, web-hosting, distance maintenance of programmes and equipment; (2) supply of software and updating thereof; (3) supply of images, text and information and making available of databases; (4) supply of music, films and games, including games of chance and gambling games, and of political, cultural, artistic, sporting, scientific and entertainment broadcasts and events; (5) supply of distance teaching.
confirms: Annex II gives an indicative ESS list that includes software supply and updating, website supply and online information or database access.
notes: The list is indicative; Article 7 of Implementing Regulation 282/2011 supplies the automation and human-intervention criteria.

title: Council Implementing Regulation (EU) No 282/2011 (consolidated), Article 7
excerpt: ‘Electronically supplied services’ as referred to in Directive 2006/112/EC shall include services which are delivered over the Internet or an electronic network and the nature of which renders their supply essentially automated and involving minimal human intervention, and impossible to ensure in the absence of information technology. […] Paragraph 1 shall not cover the following: […] (i) services of professionals such as lawyers and financial consultants, who advise clients by e-mail; (j) teaching services, where the course content is delivered by a teacher over the Internet or an electronic network (namely via a remote link);
confirms: ESS must be essentially automated, involve minimal human intervention and depend on information technology; specified professional advice and teacher-delivered remote teaching are excluded.
notes: The ellipses join verbatim, non-contiguous provisions within Article 7.

title: Council Implementing Regulation (EU) No 282/2011 (consolidated), Articles 24b and 24f
pinpoint: Article 24b, first paragraph, point (d), second paragraph; Article 24f
excerpt: (d) under circumstances other than those referred to in Article 24a and in points (a), (b) and (c) of this Article, it shall be presumed that the customer is established, has his permanent address or usually resides at the place identified as such by the supplier on the basis of two items of non-contradictory evidence as listed in Article 24f of this Regulation. Without prejudice to point (d) of the first paragraph, for supplies of services falling under that point, where the total value of such supplies, exclusive of VAT, provided by a taxable person from his business establishment or a fixed establishment located in a Member State, does not exceed EUR 100 000, or the equivalent in national currency, in the current and the preceding calendar year, the presumption shall be that the customer is established, has his permanent address or usually resides at the place identified as such by the supplier on the basis of one item of evidence provided by a person involved in the supply of the services other than the supplier or the customer, as listed in points (a) to (e) of Article 24f. […] (a) the billing address of the customer; (b) the internet Protocol (IP) address of the device used by the customer or any method of geolocation; (c) bank details such as the location of the bank account used for payment or the billing address of the customer held by that bank;
confirms: The default residual presumption uses two non-contradictory evidence items; the one-item simplification is limited to supplies from a business or fixed establishment located in a Member State and therefore does not cover a Serbian supplier with no EU establishment.
notes: Article 24f continues with SIM-country, fixed-landline and other commercially relevant evidence. The ellipsis joins Article 24b to the Article 24f evidence list.

title: European Commission VAT One Stop Shop portal — non-Union scheme, threshold and returns
issuingAuthority: European Commission, Directorate-General for Taxation and Customs Union
pinpoint: Sections ‘Background’, ‘The concept of a taxable person’ and ‘The place of supply — threshold of EUR 10 000’
excerpt: Under the non-Union scheme, a taxable person is a business (be it a company, a partnership or a sole proprietor) which has not established its business (place of business) in the EU, nor has a fixed establishment there. […] The application of this threshold is subject to the following conditions: 1. the supplier is established or, in the absence of an establishment, has his permanent address or usually resides in only one Member State […] The VAT return is submitted quarterly in the non-Union and in the Union scheme and monthly in the import scheme.
confirms: A Serbian supplier with no EU establishment is eligible for the optional non-Union OSS; the EUR 10 000 threshold conditions are unavailable to it, so destination VAT applies from the first sale, and non-Union OSS returns are quarterly.
notes: OSS is optional. Without OSS, the portal states that the supplier would be required to register in each Member State in which it supplies covered services to consumers. The ellipses join three verbatim, non-contiguous passages.

title: European Commission VIES — VAT validation and official SOAP/REST interfaces
issuingAuthority: European Commission, Directorate-General for Taxation and Customs Union
pinpoint: Technical Information, ‘REST web services’ and ‘SOAP service’
excerpt: The VoW application exposes the validation services using the Representational state transfer (REST) architectural style over the JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) format. This will allow any third-party application to verify the validity of a VAT number one by one, retrieve the status of the member state service, and test the integration of the VAT validation service. […] For the SOAP service, three WSDLs are available: […] https://ec.europa.eu/taxation_customs/vies/services/checkVatService.wsdl to verify the validity of a VAT number;
confirms: VIES is the Commission's official VAT-number validation service and publishes machine-readable REST and SOAP interfaces suitable for a server-side check.
notes: The official REST OpenAPI document is https://ec.europa.eu/assets/taxud/vow-information/swagger_publicVAT.yaml. The production REST operation is documented as /check-vat-number. The app must handle service and Member State unavailability gracefully.

title: French tax administration — standard VAT rate
excerpt: Le taux normal est applicable à toutes les opérations pour lesquelles aucun autre taux n'est prévu. Il est fixé à 20 % pour la France métropolitaine et la Corse (CGI, art. 278).
translationEn: The standard rate applies to all transactions for which no other rate is provided. It is set at 20% for metropolitan France and Corsica (CGI, Article 278).
confirms: The French standard VAT rate is 20%; TaxGraph may display it only as an illustrative rate, not as a final liability determination.
notes: This replaces the draft Légifrance URL because the task's allowlist permits impots.gouv.fr but does not list legifrance.gouv.fr.

title: German Umsatzsteuergesetz, § 13b — recipient liable for VAT
issuingAuthority: Federal Ministry of Justice / Federal Office of Justice (Germany)
excerpt: (2) Für folgende steuerpflichtige Umsätze entsteht die Steuer mit Ausstellung der Rechnung, spätestens jedoch mit Ablauf des der Ausführung der Leistung folgenden Kalendermonats: 1. Werklieferungen und nicht unter Absatz 1 fallende sonstige Leistungen eines im Ausland ansässigen Unternehmers; […] (5) In den in den Absätzen 1 und 2 Nummer 1 bis 3 genannten Fällen schuldet der Leistungsempfänger die Steuer, wenn er ein Unternehmer oder eine juristische Person ist;
translationEn: (2) For the following taxable transactions, the tax arises when the invoice is issued, but no later than the end of the calendar month following performance: 1. supplies of work and materials, and other services not falling under paragraph 1, supplied by an entrepreneur established abroad; […] (5) In the cases referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 numbers 1 to 3, the recipient owes the tax if the recipient is an entrepreneur or a legal person;
confirms: Where a taxable transaction falls within § 13b(2)(1) UStG — supplies of work and other services by an entrepreneur established abroad — § 13b(5) sentence 1 makes the recipient liable for the VAT, provided the recipient is an entrepreneur or a legal person; both conditions must be met for the reverse charge to apply.
notes: The draft pinpoint to § 13b(1) was incorrect for a Serbian (third-country) supplier; paragraph 1 concerns a supplier established elsewhere in the EU.

title: Serbian VAT Act, Article 12 — place of supply of services
excerpt: Ако се промет услуга врши пореском обвезнику, местом промета услуга сматра се место у којем прималац услуга има седиште или сталну пословну јединицу ако се промет услуга врши сталној пословној јединици која се не налази у месту у којем прималац услуга има седиште, односно место у којем прималац услуга има пребивалиште или боравиште. […] Изузетно од ст. 4. и 5. овог члана, местом промета услуга: […] 7) које се пружају лицу које није порески обвезник, сматра се место седишта, пребивалишта или боравишта примаоца услуга, ако се ради о услугама: […] (12) пружених електронским путем;
translationEn: Where services are supplied to a taxable person, the place of supply is the place where the recipient has its seat, or its fixed establishment if the services are supplied to a fixed establishment located elsewhere, or the recipient's permanent or temporary residence. […] As an exception to paragraphs 4 and 5, electronically supplied services provided to a non-taxable person are supplied at the recipient's seat, permanent residence or temporary residence.
confirms: For a Serbian seller, the B2B general rule places services at the foreign business recipient, while the listed B2C electronically supplied services follow the non-taxable recipient's location; in both scenarios the stated place is outside Serbia.
notes: Verified in the official consolidated text including amendments through Official Gazette RS 109/25, applicable from 1 April 2026 (with stated transition dates). The ellipses join the B2B rule and the B2C electronic-services exception.

title: Germany–Yugoslavia 1987 double-tax treaty, continued for Serbia — Article 13 Royalties
pinpoint: Article 13(1)–(3); BMF continuation notice for Serbia
excerpt: Royalties arising in a Contracting State and paid to a resident of the other Contracting State may be taxed in that other State. However, such royalties may also be taxed in the Contracting State in which they arise, and according to the laws of that State, but if the recipient is the beneficial owner of the royalties, the tax so charged shall not exceed 10 per cent of the gross amount of the royalties. The term ‘royalties’ as used in this Article means payments of any kind received as a consideration for the use of, or the right to use, any copyright of literary, artistic or scientific work including cinematograph films and films and tapes for television or radio broadcasting, any patent, trade mark, design or model, plan, secret formula or process, or for the use of, or the right to use, industrial, commercial, or scientific equipment, or for information concerning industrial, commercial or scientific experience.
confirms: Article 13 governs royalties and permits source-state tax up to the treaty limit where its conditions are met; whether a specific software-rights transfer is a royalty and what withholding steps apply requires professional review.
notes: The BMF country page states continued application of the German-Yugoslav treaties to Serbia: https://www.bundesfinanzministerium.de/Content/DE/Standardartikel/Themen/Steuern/Internationales_Steuerrecht/Staatenbezogene_Informationen/Laender_A_Z/Serbien/1988-09-08-Serbien-Abkommen-DBA.html. Serbia's Ministry of Finance stated on 27 June 2025 that the old treaty was still in force and that a replacement had only been initialled: https://mfin.gov.rs/sr/aktivnosti-1/parafiran-ugovor-o-izbegavanju-dvostrukog-oporezivanja-sa-nemakom-1. Keep R12 at Professional review required.
